roots.title: Introduction to Multiple Linear Regression and Dot Product
roots.summary: This segment introduces multiple linear regression, explaining how it combines multiple attributes for predictions similar to simple linear regression. It emphasizes Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) properties and evaluation methods, presenting the model equation y = ╬Ė0 + ╬Ė1x1 + ╬Ė2x2 + ... + ╬Ėpxp. Examples include predicting a cat's age based on length and weight, and NBA player scoring predictions using features like field goals and assists. The analysis of a specific player demonstrates how input features inform scoring predictions. The segment outlines four modeling stages and uses Mean Squared Error (MSE) as the loss function, focusing on model selection and parameter adjustment. Additionally, it explains the dot product of vectors, its geometric interpretation, and how predictions can be expressed as the dot product of vectors x and theta. It introduces the bias trick in machine learning and discusses the importance of dimensionality in observation vectors.

children.title: Introduction to Multiple Linear Regression Concepts and OLS
children.summary: The segment introduces multiple linear regression, detailing the process of combining multiple attributes for predictions, similar to simple linear regression. It emphasizes the properties of Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) and evaluation methods for the model. The mathematical foundation is highlighted, encouraging a review of linear algebra. The multiple linear regression model is represented by the equation y = ╬Ė0 + ╬Ė1x1 + ╬Ė2x2 + ... + ╬Ėpxp, with an example predicting a cat's age based on length and weight, and a dataset of NBA players predicting average points scored using features like field goals and assists. The analysis of a specific player illustrates how input features inform scoring predictions. Additionally, the segment outlines the four modeling stages and the use of Mean Squared Error (MSE) as the loss function, focusing on model selection and parameter adjustment for accurate predictions.

children.title: Introduction to Multiple Linear Regression Concepts
children.summary: The lecture introduces multiple linear regression, focusing on combining multiple attributes to make predictions. It outlines the modeling process, similar to simple linear regression, and covers properties of Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) along with evaluation methods for the constructed model. The segment emphasizes a math-heavy approach, encouraging students to review linear algebra concepts and watch the lecture multiple times for better understanding. The model is characterized by a linear combination of parameters (theta) weighted by feature values, maintaining linearity despite multiple terms, and utilizes a p-dimensional vector of features for predictions.

children.title: Introduction to Multiple Linear Regression
children.summary: The lecture will focus on multiple linear regression, which involves combining multiple attributes or features to make predictions. The session will outline the modeling process, similar to previous discussions on simple linear regression and constant models. Additionally, properties of Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) will be covered, along with methods for evaluating the constructed model. This marks the last lecture on linear regression before transitioning to its applications in future sessions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Good morning, everyone. Welcome to our ordinary list discourse lecture. Most of those songs had ordinary and so kind of picking the right song for today. So today is going to be the last kind of linear regression lecture that we have. And starting from next lecture, we're going to move on to a more application side of linear regression that you're seeing or you have seen so far. So kind of a teaser of today's lecture, I gave it to you in the previous lecture I talked about the fact that today we're going to do multiple linear regression And what we're going to do is that we're going to have multiple different Attributes or features that we want to combine in order to make a specific prediction So this is kind of the outline for today. I'm going to talk about Walk you through the modeling process the same forest that process that we saw previously with the simple linear regression and also with the constant model But this time we're going to see that with a multiple linear regression And then at the end we're going to talk about some of the properties of OLS or generally ceSquares and we're going to talk about evaluating the model that we have built so I'm going to start by introducing what what multiple linear regression is. children.title: Overview of Lecture Structure and Content
children.summary: The lecture will focus on reviewing and applying linear algebra concepts, emphasizing a math-heavy approach. Students are encouraged to watch the lecture multiple times to fully grasp the material, as initial understanding may be challenging. The latter part of the course will shift towards applied topics, including various machine learning algorithms and techniques.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But before diving into the content of today's lecture, I also wanna give you a warning that every section of today's lecture is just gonna be me reviewing or reminding you of some linear algebra concepts and then using them. And then I'll keep building on top of the previous concept that we built. So it's gonna be pretty linear algebra, heavy math, heavy lecture that you will see today. I'm expecting that you all will watch, re-watch this lecture at least once more, to be able to do the homework and to grasp all the concepts clearly. So just know that the first time, if some of the concepts are not super clear, that's very expected. This is kind of a very math, heavy, involved lecture. But the good news is that from this point and on, everything is gonna get easier and it's gonna be more on the applied side of the thing and then we're gonna see, In the second half of data, 100 after the meet term, we're gonna see different applications, our different machine learning algorithms or techniques every week. children.title: Introduction to Multiple Linear Regression
children.summary: Multiple linear regression combines multiple features using specific weights to predict an outcome. The model includes a y-intercept (theta 0) and parameters for each feature (theta 1 to theta p), allowing for predictions based on the weighted combination of features x1, x2, up to xp.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So things are gonna get easier, but you have to kind of get past this the specific lecture that we have. Okay, let's start. Multiple linear regression. What was that? children.title: Introduction to Multiple Linear Regression Model
children.summary: The segment introduces the multiple linear regression model, explaining that it is a linear model characterized by a linear combination of parameters (theta) weighted by the attribute values (XJ). It emphasizes that despite involving multiple terms, the function remains linear. The features, represented as a p-dimensional vector (x1, x2, ..., xp), are combined to provide different pieces of information for a single observation, which is then predicted using the weighted parameter vector.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is kind of this model that we're using. Is the linear model? Means that it is linear in terms of the teta. So it's a weighted linear combination of the teta. that those types of are weighted or scaled by each one of those XJ or each one of those attribute values that we have. That's why although we're multiplying a bunch of different terms then we're summing them all together, this is still a linear function or a linear model that we're dealing with. So in terms of the machine that we've been talking about before, what we have in multiple linear regression is that our features are going to be passed into this blue machine that I have. My features are, again, you saw x1x2 all the way to xp. It's a p-dimensional vector. If I combine all of those features together, it's going to be p different attributes and different information pieces for one single observation that I have that I'm showing that single observation using. this vector x bar, and then they are weighted using this parameter vector. children.title: Introduction to Multiple Linear Regression Concepts
children.summary: The segment introduces multiple linear regression, represented by the equation y = ╬Ė0 + ╬Ė1x1 + ╬Ė2x2 + ... + ╬Ėpxp, which predicts a single value from multiple input features. An example involving the prediction of a cat's age using its length and weight is discussed, along with a dataset of NBA players from 2018-2019. The target variable for prediction is the average points scored in basketball games, using features like average two-point field goals, assists, and three-point field goals attempted. A specific player is analyzed to illustrate how input features are used to predict scoring, emphasizing the adjustment of model parameters to align predictions with actual values.

children.title: Introduction to Multiple Linear Regression
children.summary: The segment introduces the multiple linear regression model, represented by the equation y = ╬Ė0 + ╬Ė1x1 + ╬Ė2x2 + ... + ╬Ėpxp, where the output is a single predicted value based on multi-dimensional input features. An example is provided involving the prediction of a cat's age using its length and weight over time, illustrating the collection of data points for this purpose.

children.title: Introduction to Multiple Linear Regression
children.summary: The segment introduces multiple linear regression, explaining how to use attributes like length and weight to predict outcomes, such as a cat's age. It describes calculating a weighted combination of features and mentions the use of a dataset of NBA players from 2018-2019 to explore multiple features in regression analysis.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we can see that likes just trying to stretch herself to get different lengths and then I'll collect another data point and so on and so forth. And what I'll do is I'll plug in all of these attributes that I have into a multiple regression model assuming that I have the parameters known for the weight and the length. So this is a parameter for the lengths, for example. This is a parameter for the weight. If I plug in any feature, any entry, or any observation for a cat such as, again, the lengths and the weight, what I can do is I can calculate a weighted combination of all of these features that I have. And then add on also the tetasero value and then be able to get why I had or the projection for the age. So this is kind of the simplest case that you have two features. But we're going to move on to using more features and try to scale this beyond two features and kind of generalize what kind of space we're living in with multiple regression. So today's lecture is gonna use a data set of NBA players from 2018, 2019, and we have different attributes from different players that we can use. Video: 1: Lec 12 - DS100 Fa24 - Ordinary

children.title: Predicting Average Points in Basketball Games
children.summary: The target variable for prediction is the average number of points players score in a game. Input features include the average number of two-point field goals (FG), assists (AST), and three-point field goals attempted (3PA). A dataset is structured with these features to enable predictions by calculating a weighted combination of the input parameters to estimate the target value.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The target or the target variable that we want to measure or we want to predict is the average number of points that those players are going to score in a given game. So that's the target value. That's this entire column that we have, but then we also have some input feature values that we can use. The input feature values that we have are going to be field goal. So the field goal is the number of two pointers that these players would score on average in a game. We also have a feature ASC or the number of average number of assists that they have in a given game. So this is the assists column. And then there is also a TPA or the number of tree pointers that they would score. And this is also the number of, this is exactly that feature. So you can imagine that. In a regular scenario that we have a data frame with multiple different columns, this is how we would, this is kind of the data set or the shape of the data that we want to deal with, and we want to use this information in order to be able to predict that having information about field goal assist 3-PA4, another different player can be learned parameters so that we can calculate a weighted combination of those features together in order to predict the average number of points that they would score in a given game. children.title: Multiple Linear Regression Model Overview
children.summary: The segment discusses a specific player in a dataset, detailing their features and target value of scoring an average of 0.7 points per game. It explains how a model, represented as a blue box, uses input feature vectors (e.g., average 2-point field goals, assists, and 3-point field goals attempted) to predict the player's scoring. The goal is to adjust the model parameters to ensure the prediction closely aligns with the ground truth value.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So for example, in this case, if I look at one specific row, this is the second player, what I can, what I know from this player is that this player has those specific features, and then in the ground truth, in the original dataset, but I have as the target value is that they scored an average of 0.7 points in a game. So my model is then going to look like exactly the same kind of blue machine or the blue box that I was showing you that it is parametrized by all of those parameters that I have. And then the input feature vector is going to go in and the input feature vector you can verify that this is 0.4 right here, this is 0.8 right here, and this is 1.5. And what I'm expecting is to get a prediction that hopefully this prediction why had is going to be as close as possible to this ground truth value that I have. And we're going to talk about how to build parameters that are listed here so that this prediction is as close as possible to the ground-throughs values. Video: 1: Lec 12 - DS100 Fa24 - Ordinary

children.title: Overview of Multiple Linear Regression and OLS
children.summary: The segment covers the Multiple Linear Regression Model, emphasizing its goal of accurate predictions using linear algebra. It introduces parameters (tetasero, teta1, teta2, teta3) and outlines four modeling stages, with an example relating average field goals and assists to predicting athlete performance. The focus shifts to Ordinary Least Squares (OLS), highlighting the use of Mean Squared Error (MSE) as the loss function. Key objectives include model selection, loss function definition, model fitting through least squares estimation, and performance evaluation using metrics like Root MSE and Multiple R-squared. The importance of linear algebra is noted, particularly in representing estimated optimum values with a tetra vector.

children.title: Multiple Linear Regression Model Overview
children.summary: The goal is to ensure that predictions are as close as possible to target values, using linear algebra to formulate the necessary equations. The model will include parameters (tetasero, teta1, teta2, teta3) corresponding to specific attributes. The four stages of modeling will be discussed, with the first two stages anticipated to be straightforward. An example of a multiple linear regression model is introduced, focusing on how various factors like average field goals and assists contribute to predicting an athlete's average points per game.

children.title: Ordinary Least Squares and Regression Model
children.summary: The model will focus on multiple linear regression, utilizing a different notation due to multiple features. The chosen loss function is mean squared error (MSE), which involves averaging the squares of the residuals. Today's lecture is titled 'Ordinary Least Squares,' emphasizing the multiple regression model with MSE as the loss function. Key goals include selecting the model, defining the loss function, fitting the model using calculus geometry, and evaluating performance through visualization and metrics like Root MSE and Multiple R2.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Exactly the same notation or same concepts that we have had before, but this time because we're dealing with multiple different features, we might just need to use a different notation. And that's why we will deal with matrices and so on and so forth. So we decided that the model is going to be a multiple regression model. We also decided, we will decide today for simplicity that my last function is also going to be mean squared error. Remember that you also have seen mean absolute error, You're taking the absolute value of each one of the residuals and you use that as the loss function here. We're basically taking all the residuals, we're exclering all of the residuals and we're taking the average of all of those, the squares of the residuals, which is what we're calling it as MSC or mean scored error in our case. The title of today's lecture is called Ordinary Least the Squares. And Ordinary Least the Squares means that I'm going to have a model that is multiple regression, or multiple linear regression, has multiple different features, and then the last function is minus squared error. children.title: Ordinary Least Squares Overview and Steps
children.summary: The segment discusses Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) as a general modeling approach that allows for multiple features and observations. It emphasizes the use of Mean Squared Error (MSE) as the loss function and outlines the steps involved in the modeling process: selecting a model (Multiple Linear Regression), choosing a loss function (MSE), fitting the model through least squares estimation to find optimal parameters, and evaluating the model's performance using metrics such as Root MSE and Multiple R-squared.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: That's kind of the summary of what's happening there. It's called Ordinary is because it's just the general form of the equation and you're not limiting the model to have one, into or tree specific kind of features, but the notation that we'll be using is going to be generalizable that you can true in any number of features that you have, and any number of rows that you have, and then you will be able to do the modeling. And then Mi Escort error is also going to be clear because you have target values, you have predictions you can calculate Mi Escort error between the two. Bless you. So what we will do as the turn of step is going to be doing the estimation, right? We fit the model, we have the objective function, we have the model, and we will go through a process that's called least the squares estimation in order to drive what is the optimum set of parameters, meaning that this time with this new approach, but I'm going to be after is find out the optimum tetahat vector. children.title: Introduction to Ordinary Least Squares and Matrix Representation
children.summary: The segment introduces the concept of using a tetra vector to represent estimated optimum values in multiple linear regression. It emphasizes the importance of linear algebra in formulating predictions and outlines the process of creating a single equation through matrix multiplication for various observations in a data frame. The goals of the session include selecting a model (Multiple Linear Regression), choosing a loss function (Ordinary Least Squares), fitting the model, and evaluating its performance using metrics like Mean Squared Error and Root MSE.

children.title: Transitioning to Multiple Regression and Data Structure
children.summary: The segment discusses the transition from simple linear regression (SLR) to multiple regression, highlighting the differences in model structure and calculations. In SLR, predictions use one feature vector and one target vector, while multiple regression involves multiple observations represented in a data matrix. Each row corresponds to an observation and each column to a feature, aiming to predict a target value, such as the average number of points. The importance of understanding the data matrix structure is emphasized, including how to interpret indices for observations and features. The segment concludes with an explanation of how multiple linear regression predictions are made as weighted combinations of features, with weights determined by parameters.

children.title: Transitioning from Simple to Multiple Features
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the differences between simple linear regression (SLR) and models involving multiple observations. In SLR, there is one feature vector and one target vector, allowing for straightforward calculations of predictions using scalar values. In contrast, the constant model relies solely on target values without input features. The current model involves multiple observations, represented as rows, which necessitates different methods for determining the optimal parameter estimates.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It's because the nature of my data right now is going to be different. Uh, for the simple linear regression, remember that I had one feature vector, and then I also had one target vector, uh, or one feature input column, and then one target vector column, um, that I had. And then I could directly use all of these values that I have for the features in order to get all of those predictions. and it was simply a bunch of different scalar values that I needed to deal with. So it was easy to calculate them and sum them and so on and so forth. When I was doing a constant model, there was no input feature. It was just the target values that I had for all of those YIs. And depending on the last function, we saw that there is going to be different ways of coming up with what the optimum tetasera hat value is going to be for the constant model. In this current case that we're dealing with, we have multiple different observations. So we will have those rows. Remember that here we have also row one, row two all the way to row n. leaf_chunk.audio: It's because the nature of my data right now is going to be different. Uh, for the simple linear regression, remember that I had one feature vector, and then I also had one target vector, uh, or one feature input column, and then one target vector column, um, that I had. And then I could directly use all of these values that I have for the features in order to get all of those predictions. and it was simply a bunch of different scalar values that I needed to deal with. So it was easy to calculate them and sum them and so on and so forth. When I was doing a constant model, there was no input feature. It was just the target values that I had for all of those YIs. And depending on the last function, we saw that there is going to be different ways of coming up with what the optimum tetasera hat value is going to be for the constant model. In this current case that we're dealing with, we have multiple different observations. So we will have those rows. Remember that here we have also row one, row two all the way to row n.

children.title: Multiple Regression and Data Matrix Structure
children.summary: The discussion focuses on multiple regression involving n different data points and P features. Each observation is represented as a row in a matrix, where the columns correspond to different features. The goal is to predict a target value, specifically the average number of points. The segment emphasizes understanding the structure of the data matrix and how to interpret the rows and columns, setting the stage for deriving the relevant equations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We have n different data points. Here is also the same case that we have n different data points, but it just happens that you're only keeping track of the target values that we have. In the multiple regression case, what I have is still the same scenario where I have one observation as the entire row, but the issue is that my entire row is now involved with multiple different columns that I have. And in fact, it has P different features. And I need to think about how I can combine all of the observations and all of the features for all of the observations as a matrix. And if you take a look at this, this is matrix-like. And I'm going to exactly treat that as a matrix, but we're just going to drive the actual equation or the actual notation for the equation that we'll be using. Exactly the data set that we'll be using is this data set with three different input features and a target value as the average number of points that we're trying to predict. Okay, let's see how that's going to be happening. If I'm going to think about how I will look at this matrix and what each one of those rows represent and what each one of those columns represent and what are those. children.title: Understanding Indices in Data Matrices
children.summary: Each row in a data frame represents one observation with multiple features. The first index denotes the observation number, while the second index indicates the specific feature within that observation. For example, for observation i, the attributes are labeled as feature one of observation i, feature two of observation i, and so forth, up to feature P of observation i.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: complicated indices that you see on the matrix. The way that you can think about it is that each one of the rows is one observation one row in your data frame. Right. And one row in the data frame again has multiple different features. And the way that I'm going to look at each one of these features is that, for example, I would say for observation, I. The first index of all of these attributes is always going to be I representing that this is ICE observation. But then as I'm going through multiple different features here, I would say that this is feature one of observation, i, this is feature two of observation, i, and then all the way to feature P of observation, i. So that's how you can think about how we're using all of those subscripts that we have, or those indices that we have in the matrix. So the first index or the first subscript is always going to represent which observation you're at. And then the second one or the second index represents which feature you're on. leaf_chunk.audio: complicated indices that you see on the matrix. The way that you can think about it is that each one of the rows is one observation one row in your data frame. Right. And one row in the data frame again has multiple different features. And the way that I'm going to look at each one of these features is that, for example, I would say for observation, I. The first index of all of these attributes is always going to be I representing that this is ICE observation. But then as I'm going through multiple different features here, I would say that this is feature one of observation, i, this is feature two of observation, i, and then all the way to feature P of observation, i. So that's how you can think about how we're using all of those subscripts that we have, or those indices that we have in the matrix. So the first index or the first subscript is always going to represent which observation you're at. And then the second one or the second index represents which feature you're on.

children.title: Understanding Matrix Representation in Data Frames
children.summary: The discussion focuses on how to interpret a matrix in the context of a data frame, emphasizing that each column represents a feature with consistent second index values across observations. It highlights that the first index varies, indicating different observations for the same feature. The segment also introduces multiple linear regression, explaining that predictions are made as a weighted combination of features, with weights determined by parameters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So for example, if you're looking at one entire column, what you will notice is that all of these, all of the second indices or second subscripts are exactly the same. So for example, I'm looking at the second column, second feature. So all of the second values are two. But what you're noticing here is that the first index value is one, two, all the way to end, showing that this is feature two of observation, one feature two of observation, two all the way to feature two of observation in. So that's how I want you to think about it. And if you're looking at how this matrix is going to be represented in your data frame, it's exactly as that. And every time that you had a data frame, you had rows representing observations and columns representing different attributes or different features in the data frame. And that's how we're going to treat that in the matrix word as well. So if I'm going to go ahead and write the multiple linear regression, remember that multiple linear regression was telling me that? a wall that the prediction is going to be the weighted combination of those features weighted by the parameters that I have. children.title: Multiple Linear Regression and Dot Product Calculations
children.summary: The segment covers the calculation of predictions in multiple linear regression by multiplying each feature by its corresponding weight, using predetermined TETA-I values. It explains the dot product's role in generating predictions from multiple observations, emphasizing its definition as the sum of the products of corresponding elements of two vectors of the same size. A reminder of linear algebra concepts is provided, and an example illustrates the calculation of the dot product of two three-dimensional vectors, resulting in a scalar value of 6. The segment also discusses alternative methods for calculating the dot product through matrix multiplication.

children.title: Multiple Linear Regression Predictions
children.summary: The segment discusses how to calculate predictions in multiple linear regression by multiplying each feature by its corresponding weight. The TETA-I values are set a priori, and for each observation, the features are plugged into the equation to derive predictions. The process involves taking the features from each data point and applying the regression equation to generate predictions for all observations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So for example here I would say that this is feature one that is multiplied by the weight for feature one and this is also I would say feature two that is multiplied by the weight for feature two and then all the way to feature p which was green that is multiplied by the parameter for feature P, right? So the TETA-I values are given as a priori. So you have to zero TETA-1 all the way to TET-TP. For every observation, you're basically going to take feature one feature to all the way to feature P, plug it into the equation, and we'll get the result. So effectively, what I can do is I can apply this equation to all of the rows. So I can go ahead and get the prediction for all of the observations that I have. Because this is a general form observation that I'll, that I'm showing in this case. So if I go ahead and exactly do that in order to get the prediction for data point one or observation one, what I'll do is I'll go ahead and say that get feature one of data point one, feature two of data point one all the way to feature P of data point one. leaf_chunk.audio: So for example here I would say that this is feature one that is multiplied by the weight for feature one and this is also I would say feature two that is multiplied by the weight for feature two and then all the way to feature p which was green that is multiplied by the parameter for feature P, right? For every observation, you're basically going to take feature one feature to all the way to feature P, plug it into the equation, and we'll get the result. So effectively, what I can do is I can apply this equation to all of the rows. So I can go ahead and get the prediction for all of the observations that I have. Because this is a general form observation that I'll, that I'm showing in this case. children.title: Understanding Dot Product and Predictions
children.summary: The segment discusses the process of combining features to generate predictions for multiple observations in a regression model. It emphasizes the importance of the dot product, explaining that it involves two vectors of the same size. A reminder of the dot product concept is provided, linking it to prior knowledge from linear algebra courses. The discussion transitions to summarizing the model structure, highlighting how multiple features contribute to predictions across different observations.

children.title: Calculating the Dot Product of Vectors
children.summary: The dot product, also known as the inner product, is calculated by multiplying corresponding elements of two vectors of the same length and summing the products to obtain a single scalar value. For example, to compute the dot product of two three-dimensional vectors u and v, each element is multiplied together, and the results are summed. Additionally, the dot product can be represented through matrix multiplication by transposing one vector and multiplying it with the other, exemplified by the calculation of the dot product yielding a result of 6.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then you're going to multiply corresponding elements with each other. And then you're going to take the summation of all of those products. And then the final number is going to be a scalar value number or a real value number that you can use as the dot product between two vectors. vectors. So for example in this case if I have the two vectors u and v Each one of them is three-dimensional. What I will do is in order to calculate the dot product between these two vectors, I need to multiply corresponding elements and then I'll sum up all of those products, and then it will give me a single value. So if I'm thinking about this as how to calculate exactly the dot product between the vector v and vector u, I can also think about transposing one of them and multiplying it with the other vector. So again, in this case, this would be equal to saying that, okay, I'm going to transpose, for example, the vector u, this is going to give me 1, 2, 3, and then I'm going to multiply it with the column vector 1, 1, 1, 1. This is very simple, 1 row, 1 row vector, 1 column vector, so you would just do the normal matrix multiplication, which is going to give you 1 times 1 plus 2 times 1 plus 3 times 1, which is equal to 6. children.title: Dot Product Definition and Calculation Methods
children.summary: The dot product, or inner product, is defined as a vector operation that can only be performed on two vectors of the same length. It involves summing the products of corresponding entries of the two vectors, resulting in a single number. An example calculation shows that the dot product of vectors u and v equals 6. Additionally, an alternative method to calculate the dot product is presented, which involves the magnitudes of the vectors and the cosine of the angle between them, emphasizing that the dot product can be understood in terms of these geometric properties.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And in fact, if you were to actually do it exactly, but just doing the element wise product and the do the summation, you would also get the value 6 as a result. Something that I'll use later in today's lecture is that another way to think about the dot product is that you can calculate the dot product by This formula that the dot product between the vector u and vector v is equal to calculating the magnitude of or calculating the product of the magnitude or the length of each of those vectors u and v. and then times cosine of the angle between the two vectors. I'm going to talk about why that's going to be helpful, but just take that without proof from me that the dot product you can either calculate it or you can think about the dot product as being calculating the dot product between two vectors as being the lengths of one vector times lengths of another vector times they co-sign at the angle between the two. children.title: Dot Product, Multiple Regression, and Matrix Notation
children.summary: The segment explains the dot product of vectors, calculated by multiplying corresponding entries and summing them to yield a scalar. It highlights the geometric interpretation of the dot product as the product of vector magnitudes and the cosine of the angle between them. The discussion includes optimizing vector calculations for predictions by consolidating parameters into a single vector and introduces Tetra 0 multiplication. It also covers multiple regression, expressing predictions as the dot product of vectors x and theta, computed using the transpose of x multiplied by theta. The segment illustrates how predictions can be aggregated with a common theta vector, using an NBA dataset as an example, and introduces the bias trick in machine learning, where an augmented bias is added to the model for representation as a dot product. Lastly, it addresses the importance of dimensionality in observation vectors, noting that p features lead to a dimensionality of p + 1 due to the bias term.

children.title: Calculating and Understanding Dot Product of Vectors
children.summary: The dot product, or inner product, of two vectors of the same length is calculated by multiplying corresponding entries and summing the results, yielding a scalar value. It can be expressed mathematically for vectors u = (u1, u2, u3) and v = (v1, v2, v3) as u1*v1 + u2*v2 + u3*v3. Geometrically, the dot product represents the product of the magnitudes of both vectors and the cosine of the angle between them. The discussion also covers optimizing vector calculations for predictions by consolidating multiple parameters into a single vector for efficient computation. Additionally, a method for Tetra 0 multiplication is introduced, involving a column vector paired with another vector starting with one, demonstrating that their dot product equals the summation at the top.

children.title: Calculating the Dot Product of Vectors
children.summary: The dot product, or inner product, is calculated by multiplying corresponding entries of two vectors of the same length and summing the results. For vectors u = (u1, u2, u3) and v = (v1, v2, v3), the dot product is computed as u1*v1 + u2*v2 + u3*v3. Additionally, the dot product can be interpreted geometrically as the product of the magnitudes of both vectors and the cosine of the angle between them.

children.title: Understanding the Dot Product in Vectors
children.summary: The dot product, or inner product, is a vector operation performed on two vectors of the same length, summing the products of their corresponding entries to yield a single number. An example illustrates this with a calculation showing that the dot product of two vectors results in a scalar value. Additionally, the geometric interpretation of the dot product involves the magnitudes of the vectors and the cosine of the angle between them, providing deeper insight into its significance.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Why am I writing it with this notation is because I want you to notice that every time you've seen that your calculating is summation of The stuff that they are the same set of elements that are multiplied together but with different indices you can think about two vectors being kind of that you're calculating the dot product between those two vectors. And this is kind of an intuition behind how you can identify when a dot product is being calculated. The place that I'm going to use that trick is going to be here. If you take a look at what I have in terms of for the part that I have tet 1 times x1, tet 2 times x still the way to tet p times xp, that's as if that there was a vector of tet values and there was a vector of x values and I'm calculating the dog product between the two. equal to 10 to 0 plus the dot for like between those two vectors that I have. children.title: Optimizing Vector Calculations for Predictions
children.summary: The discussion focuses on simplifying the calculation of predictions from data sets with numerous features by optimizing and parallelizing vector operations. The speaker explains the process of calculating the product between two vectors and summing additional terms. The aim is to consolidate the calculations into a single dot product format, integrating all parameters into one vector for efficiency. The challenge arises from associating each parameter with its corresponding feature, which leads to the collection of all parameters into a single vector for streamlined computation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So it's going to be much simpler. Imagine that you will have data sets with millions of different features and you don't want to calculate those products manually and then some of all of those products manually in order to calculate in order to just do a simple prediction. Let alone doing optimization later on that you will see that it's going to be more complicated. So here I'm calculating the product between two vectors. This can be optimized and parallelized, and then I'm just summing up tetasero as a second term. But still, I'm going to be a little bit greedy and I would say that I want everything to just be written as the duck product between two vectors. I don't want to have one duck product plus the summation. Is there a way that I can move pet to zero also into the duck product calculation that I have. The issue that I wasn't able to do it initially was that each one of these tetan- tetan- i values they had kind of a partner. This one had a partner of x1, this one had a partner of x2, this one had a partner of xp, so that's why I was able to just collect all the partners into one column vector x and then all of the tetan- values into tetan vector. children.title: Using Vector Notation for Tetra 0 Multiplication
children.summary: The discussion involves a method to handle the multiplication of Tetra 0 by introducing a partner value of one. By aggregating Tetra 0 values into a column vector and pairing them with another vector that starts with one followed by the x values, the dot product of these two vectors can be shown to equal the summation at the top of the discussion. This concept emphasizes the relationship between vector notation and dot products.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But then what I didn't have for Tetra 0 was that kind of partner that multiplied 2 tetra 0. But I can use a trick. What I can do is I can say that what if I assume that theta zero was multiplied by one? Which in fact it was, right? and just use one as that Hrthner that is supposedly missing. And in this case, effectively, if I'm going to aggregate all of the Teta 0 or Teta i values into a column vector, and aggregate all of the partners into another vector, this other vector is going to have the value 1, and then x1, and then x2, and then xp, and so on and so forth, which is going to give me exactly this. Bye. I'm just going to leave you with that to think about it for a sec. and just verify that the dot product between those two column vectors in fact is equal to that summation that i have at the top because if you calculate the dot product between or if you calculate the If you... children.title: Multiple Regression, Dot Product, and Matrix Notation
children.summary: The segment covers the multiplication of vectors element-wise to derive the multiple regression equation, expressing predictions as the dot product of vectors x and theta. It explains that the dot product can be computed using the transpose of x multiplied by theta, which is crucial for making predictions across all data points. Each data row is represented as the dot product of the observation transposed and the theta vector, simplifying calculations. The discussion includes how predictions can be aggregated using a common theta vector across multiple observations, illustrated with an NBA dataset example. Additionally, the bias trick in machine learning is introduced, where an augmented bias is added to the model, allowing for representation as a dot product of vectors, with specific calculations involving the theta vector.

children.title: Multiple Regression and Dot Product Notation
children.summary: The segment discusses the multiplication of vectors element-wise to derive the multiple regression equation. It explains how to express the prediction task in terms of the dot product between two vectors, x and theta. The dot product can be calculated using the transpose of x multiplied by theta, resembling normal matrix multiplication. The speaker emphasizes the importance of this approach for calculating predictions across all data points in a dataset.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Can I multiply element wise these two vectors? You're multiplying that to zero with one, that to one with x one, that to two with x two and so on and so forth. And then obviously that's going to give you your multiple regression equation. to just write our prediction task or that multiple regression equation in terms of the dot product between two vectors, x and theta, which we know that the dot product between x, the vector x and also the vector theta can be calculated using transposing x and multiplying it with theta as a normal matrix multiplication. So that's theta, this is x, and then we'll be on our way. So what I can do is I can actually go ahead and apply exactly the same thing to all of these predictions that I wanted to do for uh, remember that for data point one for data point two for data point and all of the rows that I had in the data frame I had to calculate this long summation individually. children.title: Matrix Notation for Predictions
children.summary: Each row of data can be represented as the dot product of the observation transposed and the theta vector, simplifying the summation notation for predictions. The prediction for each data point is calculated by transposing its observation and multiplying it with the theta vector, allowing for a more condensed and parallelizable approach to the calculations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But now I will do is I can actually say that each one of these rows is going to be equal to. The observation transposed times tetatovector or the dot product between that observation and the tetatovector that I have. In fact... That is a shortened version of writing all of those summations. So the prediction for data point 1 in this case is going to be just transposing the observation for data point 1 and just multiplying that with the 10th of vector that we have. And then you follow exactly the same thing for all of the other data points. And it's actually nice, condensed, it can be parallelized and that's an easier version of writing the entire long summation that we have. Okay, how are we doing so far? children.title: Matrix Notation for Predictions in Data Analysis
children.summary: Predictions are calculated by transposing data points and multiplying them with a common theta vector across multiple observations. The speaker emphasizes the ability to aggregate rows and multiply them with a single theta vector. Using an NBA dataset as an example, the speaker illustrates how to make predictions based on specific feature values, demonstrating the relationship between observations and target values in matrix notation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is the same kind of equation that we had for each one of those observations, for each one of those predictions that I wanted to do. I am calculating those predictions using transposing the data point, multiplying it with the tetavector. What I want you to notice is that this heta vector is going to be a vector that's in common between all of those rows. So why am I going to separate them into multiple different equations? Uh. multiple equations in the case that I could also aggregate all of those rows together and then multiply all of those rows together with the single petal vector that they have in common Again, if you take a look at what's happening with the NBA data set, so this is exactly the single observation that I have with three different features, and this is the target value that I have. And in order for me to make a prediction, what I can do is I can say that my observation is now going to be 0.4, 0.4 right here. Video: 1: Lec 12 - DS100 Fa24 - Ordinary

children.title: Bias Trick and Matrix Notation in Calculations
children.summary: The segment discusses the concept of the bias trick in machine learning, where an augmented bias is added to the model. This allows for the representation of the model as a dot product of vectors, facilitating calculations. The target value is set at 1.7, and the calculations involve the theta vector, which includes theta 0, theta 1, theta 2, and theta 3. The segment also introduces matrix notation for data points, illustrating how each observation is represented and how the calculations can be structured in a matrix format.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: 0.8, 0.8 right here as a feature and then 1.5, 1.5 right here. And then notice here that I have this one, which is the augmented bias that I added. And this is in fact called a bias trick because the trick that I use in order to find that to zero kind of company or something as the value one that then I Combined it with all the all the rest of the X's or the features that then I can have everything written as the doc product Within two vectors. This is called a bias trick And this entry is actually going to be related to a feature that's simulating what's going to be multiplied by the tetah 0. My target value is obviously based on what I have here is going to be 1.7, and if I have the tetah vector with all of those tetah values, tetah 0, tetah 1, tetah 2, and tetah 3, then I can go ahead and calculate the dog product between X2 and tetah, or calculate X2 transcos times tetah and get all of these values. children.title: Dimensionality and Matrix Multiplication in Predictions
children.summary: The dimensionality of observation vectors is important, with p features leading to a dimensionality of p + 1 due to the bias term. For example, with three features, the dimensionality is 4. The theta vector also has a dimensionality of 4. Predictions are calculated by transposing the augmented observation and multiplying it with the theta vector, yielding a scalar output for each data point. By combining transposed input values into a matrix, predictions for all data points can be generated efficiently, resulting in a y hat vector that consolidates all predictions. A design matrix is used, consisting of n rows and p + 1 columns, where one column is all ones and the others represent features, facilitating efficient predictions through matrix multiplication.

children.title: Dimensionality of Vectors in Observations
children.summary: The dimensionality of observation vectors is crucial for understanding the data. For an observation with p features, the dimensionality is p + 1, accounting for the bias term. In the given example, with three features (field goal, assist, TPA), the dimensionality is 4. The theta vector also has a dimensionality of p + 1, which in this case equals 4. The segment emphasizes the importance of recognizing this dimensionality in data analysis.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: One thing that I want you to notice is the dimensionality of all of these vectors that I'm dealing with. Because this dimensionality is going to be very helpful moving on. So the dimensionality of x2 or the observation, I'm saying that it's in r4 or is in the 4 dimensional space. It is correct because I have three different features, field goal assist and TPA, and then I added one more dimension for this bias term that I added right here. So always if you have p different features, the dimensionality of your observations is going to be p plus 1. He attributes for p different attributes that you obviously have for any observation and then one for the bias trick or the bias term that you're adding on top of that. The tetah vector is always going to be p plus 1 dimensional. So for example, in this case, it's going to be equal to 4 because I have 3 features plus the bias that to 0 that makes 4 different parameters. Video: 1: Lec 12 - DS100 Fa24 - Ordinary

children.title: Prediction Calculation Using Matrix Notation
children.summary: The prediction for a data point is calculated by transposing the augmented observation, which includes a bias term, and multiplying it with the theta vector. This method can be applied to all data points to derive their predictions efficiently, as the theta vector remains constant across the calculations. The output is a scalar value, indicating that the model predicts a single value rather than a vector.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So it's always going to be p plus 1 again if you have p different features. And always the target value and then the prediction value is going to be a real value number. So it's just the scalar value that we're predicting as the output. for not predicting a vector or predicting a single value. Because that's what the dot product is going to give you at the end. Okay. So let's take a look at what's happening here. These are all the equations that I had. I was saying that in order to get the prediction for data point one, I can go ahead and transpose the augmented observation that noticed that is augmented with this bias trick or this additional dimension that I added, multiplied with the tetavector that I have. I can write exactly the same thing for all of the different data points that I have, such as Y2 and then Y2 hat and then YN hat and get all the predictions for all of the different vectors. are different rows that I have. One thing that I told you before is that if you notice that this TETA vector is the same or is as common between all of those rows and instead of me calculating all of those values independently. children.title: Matrix Multiplication for Predictions
children.summary: Combining transposed input values into a matrix allows for multiplication with a theta vector, resulting in predictions for each row of the matrix. This process generates a y hat vector, which consolidates all predictions into a single output.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What if I combine all of these x i transpose values into a matrix and just go ahead and multiply that matrix by the same TETA vector that all of those rows are going to be multiplied to. So in that case, if I'm treating this as an entire matrix, the matrix multiplication by default is going to go and multiply the first row with the tetavector and then it's going to go ahead and multiply the second row with the tetavector and so on and so forth. Right. So that's a normal practice that I can do. So that's exactly what I'll do. What I'll get as a result of this matrix multiplication is that for each one of those rows that are multiplied by the theta vector, I'm getting a prediction. So I'm just going to stack all of those predictions together and I'm going to get a y vector, a y hat vector. So all of those predictions are going to be in one place. leaf_chunk.audio: What if I combine all of these x i transpose values into a matrix and just go ahead and multiply that matrix by the same TETA vector that all of those rows are going to be multiplied to. So in that case, if I'm treating this as an entire matrix, the matrix multiplication by default is going to go and multiply the first row with the tetavector and then it's going to go ahead and multiply the second row with the tetavector and so on and so forth. Right. So that's a normal practice that I can do. So that's exactly what I'll do. What I'll get as a result of this matrix multiplication is that for each one of those rows that are multiplied by the theta vector, I'm getting a prediction. So I'm just going to stack all of those predictions together and I'm going to get a y vector, a y hat vector. So all of those predictions are going to be in one place.

children.title: Design Matrix and Predictions Using Matrix Multiplication
children.summary: A design matrix is created with n rows and p plus one columns, where one column consists of all ones and p columns represent different features used for predictions. This matrix allows for efficient predictions through matrix multiplication, encapsulating all n equations into a single matrix equation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: this was exactly your data frame right you just augmented it with one column of all of these one values. Once you have the TETA vector, you can just plug in the TETA vector right here. And you would multiply your entire... your entire data frame with that set of vector and as a result you're going to get all of those predictions in one shot in one vector. Right, so very, very kind of pretty way of doing all the predictions all advanced using a simple matrix multiplication. This matrix that I created noticed that it has n rows and it has p plus one different columns. And for each one of the observations obviously because I'm stacking them together and then p plus one for the number of columns because there is one column for all of those values of ones and then p for p different features that we are using in order to make the prediction. So. What we're calling this matrix that I created is we're calling it a design matrix. Video: 1: Lec 12 - DS100 Fa24 - Ordinary

children.title: Design Matrix, Dimensionality, and Predictions in Regression
children.summary: The design matrix for modeling is defined as an n x (p + 1) matrix, where n is the number of observations and p is the number of features, including a bias column of ones. The dimensionality of the parameter vector is (p + 1) by 1, allowing for predictions to be represented as an n by 1 column vector. The objective is to minimize the distance between prediction and ground truth vectors, with perfect prediction being theoretically unattainable. The segment introduces multiple linear regression using matrix notation, illustrating how the model is expressed as a weighted combination of parameters, and includes a question regarding linear expressions in theta.

children.title: Design Matrix and Dimensionality in Modeling
children.summary: The design matrix used for modeling is a matrix of dimensions n x (p + 1), where n represents the number of observations and p the number of features. This matrix includes a bias column filled with ones, corresponding to the MBA dataset. Each row represents an observation, and each column represents a feature, including the bias term. The segment emphasizes the importance of the design matrix in linear algebra for representing predictions across all data points.

The only thing that I did was I added a bias column here with all the values of ones and everything else is the same. So. That's exactly this design matrix right here where each one of those rows is one observation each one of those columns is a feature and there's always a column that corresponds to bias or that bias trick that I add. children.title: Dimensionality of Design Matrix and Parameter Vector
children.summary: The dimensionality of the design matrix is established as n by (p + 1), where n is the number of observations and p is the number of features. Each feature requires a corresponding parameter, plus an additional parameter for the bias term, resulting in a parameter vector of dimension (p + 1) by 1. When multiplying the design matrix by the parameter vector, the output dimensionality is confirmed to be n by 1, aligning with the prediction vector.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So the dimensionality of this design matrix is always going to be n by p plus 1. This is what we already established. What is the dimensionality of the tetavector that we have? So we need to have one parameter corresponding to each one of those features. So we said that we have p different features because our features are going to be feature one feature to all the way to feature p. So we need to have one parameter corresponding to each one of those features. And then also we need to have a parameter for tet 0 for the bias term that we're adding in the model. So the dimensionality of my tetavector is then going to be a column vector of P plus 1 dimensional. p features plus a biester so you're looking at that if you multiply the design matrix with the parameter vector what will be the dimensionality of the output or the result If you multiply a matrix as n by p plus 1 Buy a vector that's P plus 1 by 1. It would be. and by one. And that's exactly what we're getting. Video: 1: Lec 12 - DS100 Fa24 - Ordinary

children.title: Column Vectors in Predictions and Ground Truth
children.summary: A column vector is created containing predictions for each data point in a dataset, which matches the dimensions of a ground truth vector of scalar values. The aim is to minimize the distance between these two vectors, ideally achieving a distance of zero for perfect predictions, although perfect prediction is acknowledged as unattainable in practice. The lecture also introduces the multiple linear regression model using matrix notation, outlining how to express the linear model across the entire dataset.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we're getting a column vector that has end different dimensions. And those end values that we have are all of the predictions that we want for each one of those data points in our data set or each one of the observations that we want. This is how the dimensions do also match with each other. So we have a column vector of all of these predictions that we have. And then our ground truth is also a column vector of all of these, all of these scalar values that we have. So we have two vectors of the same dimension. So we can calculate the distance between the two vectors. the predictions that I have. And this is a vector for all of the predictions that I have. I want to make sure that these two vectors are as close to each other as possible. So the best case scenario is that the distance between them is equal to zero. So I have perfect production. But in reality, we know that we can get perfect prediction. Video: 1: Lec 12 - DS100 Fa24 - Ordinary

children.title: Linear Models and Matrix Notation in Regression
children.summary: A multiple regression model, referred to as a linear model, can be expressed as a weighted combination of parameters (theta). This allows the model to be represented in matrix notation, where the parameter vector is multiplied by a design matrix. The segment includes a slider question asking which of several expressions are linear in theta, emphasizing that an expression is linear in theta if it is a linear combination of the parameters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But what we want to make sure is that the distance between these two is minimized. And that's what we're going to do in the next step. Before I go to the next step, this is a slider question for you. We talked a lot about, we have this multiple regression model that we call it a linear model. We had seen that a multiple regression model... or a linear model is a model that is a weighted combination of tetas, meaning that we can factor out the tetah vector as one vector and then multiply all the weights and all of the parameters or features as a separate matrix to it. And in that case we would call a model, a linear model in tetah. So the question for you is that which one of these five options are linear in TETTA based on the description that I gave you. And I will stay here. I'm a lot of your response. Video: 1: Lec 12 - DS100 Fa24 - Ordinary

leaf_chunk.audio: But what we want to make sure is that the distance between these two is minimized. And that's what we're going to do in the next step. Before I go to the next step, this is a slider question for you. We talked a lot about, we have this multiple regression model that we call it a linear model. We had seen that a multiple regression model... or a linear model is a model that is a weighted combination of tetas, meaning that we can factor out the tetah vector as one vector and then multiply all the weights and all of the parameters or features as a separate matrix to it. And in that case we would call a model, a linear model in tetah. So the question for you is that which one of these five options are linear in TETTA based on the description that I gave you. children.title: Linear Expressions and Regression Models Overview
children.summary: Expressions are evaluated for linearity in theta, defined as linear combinations of parameters, represented as dot products of vectors. A poll identifies linear expressions, emphasizing that while features can be non-linear, the parameter vector must remain linear. The linear model in regression is structured as a design matrix multiplied by a theta vector for predictions, focusing on minimizing the distance between predicted and actual values. The mean-squared error (MSE) is introduced as a loss function, highlighting the importance of notation in measuring norms and providing a reminder of linear algebra concepts.

children.title: Understanding Linear Expressions in Theta
children.summary: Expressions are evaluated for linearity in theta, where an expression is considered linear if it can be represented as a linear combination of parameters. The first expression is confirmed to be linear in theta, as it can be expressed as a dot product between two vectors: one containing all theta values and another with other relevant values. The discussion includes a poll to identify which expressions are linear in theta, with examples provided for analysis.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Let's take a look. You're saying form five. Form five are obvious because they take exactly the same form of our linear regression, right? So they have a matrix that is multiplied by a vector of all of those theta values that I have. But we can go and take a look at one, two, and three, and then see which one of them are linear in tetan, which one of them are not linear in tetan. So it's starting with the first one. So the first one is linear in tetan because I can write it as the dot product between the two vectors and the two vectors are one vector fully have all the tetan values and non-transform tetan values. values and then another vector has all of those other values that I don't care if they're linear, they're not linear and whatever value they have. The only thing that I care about is that I can write my equation in a way that all the tetas values can be factored out into a vector. children.title: Understanding Linear Expressions in Theta
children.summary: An expression is considered 'linear in theta' if it can be expressed as a linear combination of parameters, allowing for separation into a matrix product of two terms: a vector of thetas and a matrix/vector that does not involve thetas. The discussion highlights that while features can be non-linear, the parameter vector must remain linear and untransformed. An example illustrates this concept, showing how different features can be combined, such as using the product of two features instead of them separately.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And 2 is also linear in theta. Because again, it doesn't matter. That, for example, in this case, I have theta 2. And then I'm multiplying theta 2 with x2 and x3. That doesn't matter. What is happening, basically, is that when you're looking at your featured 2, so this is the bias term, this is feature 1, and this is feature 2, it just happens that you did feature in generating, that you're going to see, I think, next lecture, two lectures from now. The widget feature engineering and we decided that instead of using x2 and x3 separately, I'm gonna use the product of those two terms together as a feature. So the features can be non-linear, but the parameter vector should stay intact and should stay linear and untransformed. What we're seeing in the last one is that last one is not... Linear in tetra there is no way that you can just factor out all the tethers into one vector and be able to create This term of tetra 3 times multiply with tetra 4 so only that the The third option is the only option that's not linear in KETTA. children.title: Linear Model and Predictions in Regression
children.summary: The model is described as linear in theta, meaning it can be expressed as a linear combination of parameters organized into a design matrix multiplied by a theta vector to generate predictions. The next step involves minimizing the distance between the predicted values and the actual ground-truth values. This segment emphasizes the formulation of the multiple regression model and the significance of the design matrix in making predictions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So, kind of to set up the expectation that when we say that the model is linear in KETTA, what we mean exactly is that collect everything else into a matrix. every time that you want to do prediction. Uh or you want to prediction on your data set you would just create your design matrix you would multiply it with the tetha vector and then you would get all the predictions that you want in the output. So the next piece. This is the second step of the modeling. Now we established what our model is. In fact, our model, as we talked about, is exactly this equation. So my multiple regression model is that why how does always equal to design matrix multiplied with the test of vector? The next step that I need to do is go ahead and and minimize the distance between all the predictions and all the ground-trust values y. Video: 1: Lec 12 - DS100 Fa24 - Ordinary

children.title: Mean Squared Error in Linear Regression
children.summary: The segment discusses the mean-squared error (MSE) as a loss function for multiple linear regression models. It emphasizes the significance of the notation used to measure norms, specifically the double vertical lines around the difference between actual and predicted values (y - y hat). A reminder of linear algebra concepts is provided, particularly focusing on the subscript notation in the context of MSE. The segment sets the stage for further exploration of MSE's geometric interpretation and its role in evaluating model performance.

children.title: L1 and L2 Norms, Mean Squared Error, and OLS Regression
children.summary: The segment explains L1 and L2 norms, focusing on the L2 norm as a measure of vector size, error assessment, and distance between vectors. It details the calculation of the L2 norm for n-dimensional vectors and its application in evaluating the difference between predicted and actual values. The discussion includes mean squared error (MSE) as a squared L2 norm derived from residuals, emphasizing its mathematical formulation and significance in minimizing error. The relationship between MSE and L2 norms is highlighted, alongside the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression objectives, which aim to minimize MSE to enhance model predictions by ensuring that predicted values closely match true values.

children.title: Understanding L1 and L2 Norms and Their Applications
children.summary: The norm of a vector quantifies its size or length, with L1 and L2 norms being key types. The L2 norm for an n-dimensional vector is calculated as the square root of the sum of the squares of its components, denoted with double vertical lines and a subscript 2. It serves as a measure of errors by comparing predicted and actual values and generalizes the Pythagorean theorem for n-dimensional space. The L2 norm can also measure the distance between two vectors through their differences. In practical applications, the L2 norm is used to assess the distance between predicted and ground-truth values in datasets by calculating the L2 norm of the residual vector, which aids in minimizing error.

children.title: Understanding Vector Norms: L1 and L2 Norms
children.summary: The norm of a vector measures its size or length, with L1 and L2 norms being the primary types discussed. The L2 norm for an n-dimensional vector x, represented as x1 to xn, is calculated as the square root of the sum of the squares of its components. The notation for the L2 norm uses double vertical lines with a subscript 2, indicating the second order norm. The L1 norm will be defined in a future discussion.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: When we want to measure the lengths or size of a vector, we use norm of a vector. There are different norms for a vector. We have L1 norm, we have L2 norm. You've seen L1 loss and L2 losses before, when we were calculating minus squared error, or mean absolute error. So that's kind of for both of the L1 and L2 loss, that was kind of a measure of the distance or the length of the error vector that you have. In a general sense, if I have an n-dimensional vector, so here I have an n-dimensional vector x with the values x1x to all the way to xn. The L2 norm of this vector is going to be a square root of each one of the summation of x i is squares. Okay, so this is again a square root of the summation of all of these x i squares that I have over all of the dimensions that I have. Every time that I want to show L2 norm, I'll show it with this double vertical line and then the subscript 2 is showing you that you're taking the second order or the L2 norm or you're calculating a length based on L2 norm. children.title: Understanding L2 Norm and Its Applications
children.summary: The L2 norm measures the size or length of a vector and is conceptually similar to the squared error function. It can be interpreted as a score of errors when considering the differences between predicted and actual values. The L2 norm also generalizes the Pythagorean theorem, providing a mathematical framework for understanding vector lengths in n-dimensional space. In this course, both L1 and L2 norms are important, with a focus on L2 norms for the current discussion.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Okay, one thing that you might notice here, again, try to build intuition from everything that you know and every time that you're looking at the equation or some objective function. I'm talking about l2Norm here. The format of what you're seeing in the right hand side of the value for the l2Norm is kind of similar to the squared error function that you had seen before. So if you were thinking about X-I as being errors, and if you go ahead and score the errors and sum up all of the scores of the errors, this is kind of telling you that this is kind of a score of the errors, or we can use this notation to calculate the scores of the errors, the summation of the scores of the errors. Right, I would say summation of the scores of the errors. But let's see how we can use it in practice. So this L2 norm can be used in order to, in order to, again, measure the lengths or size of a vector. And it's also kind of a generalization of Pythagorean theorem. children.title: L2 Norm and Distance Between Vectors
children.summary: The L2 norm generalizes the Pythagorean theorem to measure the length of vectors in n dimensions. For a vector with components A and B, the length is calculated as the square root of A squared plus B squared. Additionally, the L2 norm can be used to measure the distance between two vectors by considering the vector difference A minus B.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So in Pythagorean term, you had that, okay, if one side is A, one side is B, this other side is going to have the lengths of a school root of A squared plus B squared. But we also know is that we can use and generalize this notation and say that, okay, if you have a vector of A and B, which means that across the x dimension, you have A units of distance across the y dimension, you have B units of distance. The length of this vector from this equation is exactly going to be a score, which have A squared plus B squared. So kind of, it will tell you that this notion is going to help you measure the length of a vector. In addition to measuring the lengths of a vector, it can also help you measure the distance between two vectors. So for example, again, if I have two vectors here A and B, in order for me to measure the distance between A and B, effectively I can say that this vector right here is going to be the vector of A minus B. children.title: Calculating L2 Norm for Residuals
children.summary: The L2 norm is used to measure the distance between two vectors, specifically the predictions and the ground-truth values in a dataset. By calculating the L2 norm of the residual vector, which is derived by subtracting the ground-truth values from the predictions, one can quantify the loss. This process is similar to calculating the objective function for minimizing error, as it involves summing the squared residuals.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Right? That's the distance or the delta between A and B. And in order for me to measure the length of that A-B vector, I can calculate L2 norm of that vector A-B, which is what I wrote exactly right here. Why is it going to be helpful? Because remember that I said I have a vector of all the predictions, and I have the vector of all of the ground-trust values. And I want to calculate the distance between the two. So I can actually calculate the distance between the two by stop tracking one from the other. Which is going to give me all the residual values or is going to give me the residual vector, which then I can go ahead and calculate L2 norm of that residual vector and just measure the lengths of that residual vector in order to figure out how much loss I have and also write the the minuscord error objective function based on that value. Again, this is the point that I was making that the entire summation of this x i squared values is kind of similar to what you're what you had seen in the objective function of calculating minus score there. children.title: Mean Squared Error, L2 Norms, and OLS Interpretation
children.summary: The segment covers the calculation and significance of mean squared error (MSE) as a squared L2 norm, explaining how it is derived from the residuals between predicted and actual values. It details the process of summing squared residuals and dividing by the number of observations to express MSE mathematically. The relationship between MSE and L2 norms is emphasized, along with the calculation of the L2 norm of the delta vector. The discussion includes minimizing the cost function by focusing on the L2 norm of the squared residual vector, illustrating how this leads to optimal predictions without vector calculus. Finally, it interprets the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) objective function, highlighting the goal of minimizing MSE to reduce the distance between true and predicted values.

children.title: Mean Squared Error and L2 Norms
children.summary: The segment discusses the calculation of the mean squared error (MSE) as a squared L2 norm. It emphasizes that the MSE can be expressed using the notation for the L2 norm, where predictions are compared to actual values by calculating residuals. The process involves summing the squared residuals and dividing by the number of observations (n). The relationship between the MSE and the L2 norm is highlighted, reinforcing the connection between these concepts in the context of evaluating model performance.
