children.title: Mean Squared Error and L2 Norm Calculation
children.summary: The segment discusses calculating the L2 norm of the delta vector, which represents the residuals between predicted values (y-hat) and actual values (y). The L2 norm is squared to eliminate the square root, leading to the formulation of mean squared error as a squared L2 norm. This is expressed mathematically as the summation of the squared differences between actual and predicted values, demonstrating the relationship between mean squared error and L2 norms.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I have a vector of all of the prediction values. Go ahead and calculate L to norm. of this delta vector. So this is my delta vector. Go ahead and calculate the L2 norm. But because this L2 norm has this square root and we want to get rid of that, we will is square it one more time. So let me clean this up. and let's take a look at it one more time. So again, I have all the y-i's, I have all of the y-i hats, I need to calculate residuals and and the score the residuals. The place that I can get this summation is exactly going to be this l2 norm of this vector and then is square this l2 norm because I don't have a square within this case. So then again what's happening is that this is going to be this summation right here is going to be equal to me taking l to norm of y minus y hat vector. This is L2 norm, so that's where the subscript is coming from, and then taking it to the power of 2 so that I can get rid of the square root. Video: 1: Lec 12 - DS100 Fa24 - Ordinary

children.title: Minimizing Cost Function with L2 Norms
children.summary: The objective function for the prediction task involves calculating the factor of 1 over n multiplied by the L2 norm of the squared residual vector. This means minimizing the length of the residual vector, which is essential for achieving predictions that closely align with target values. Instead of using vector calculus, a geometric derivation will be employed to illustrate how the estimated parameter vector (theta hat) minimizes the cost function, leading to optimal predictions.

So that's why the entire objective function is going to be 1 over n times L2 norm is square of the residual vector. Okay, this is going to be very simple. Uh, if condensed, so the entire prediction task wasn't make your multiplication. The entire objective function is that you're calculating one over n times the L2N on the square of a delta vector. So you have a vector, you just calculate the length of this vector, and your objective function is going to be a function of that. And everything is going to go from there. So what I want to do... is to make sure that the length of this vector is minimized. children.title: Interpreting the Ordinary Least Squares Objective Function
children.summary: The segment discusses the interpretation of the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) problem and its objective function. It emphasizes minimizing the mean squared error for the linear model, minimizing the distance between true and predicted values, and minimizing the length of the residual vector. The options presented include A: minimizing mean squared error, B: minimizing distance between true and predicted values, C: minimizing the length of the residual vector, D: all of the above, and E: something else.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Okay. So let's take a look at this objective function one more time and this is the second slide of question. What I want you to think about is that based on all this expression expressions that I showed you the objective function, however also making predictions. What are we exactly doing in the OLS problem? How do we interpret this objective function? Let's take a look at those options that we have. Minimizing miniscord error, minimizing the gussets, minimizing the lengths, so on and so forth. Let me know. children.title: OLS Regression Objectives and Model Fitting
children.summary: The segment covers the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) problem in linear regression, focusing on minimizing the mean squared error of the model defined as Y = X╬Ė. It highlights the goal of ensuring predicted values closely match true values and discusses minimizing the length of the residual vector, e = Y - ┼Č. The objective function is defined as one over n times the square of the residual vector's length, indicating that minimizing this length also minimizes the residuals. The interpretation of OLS includes minimizing mean squared error and distances between true and predicted values. A two-minute break is announced before proceeding with calculations and geometric interpretations. Additionally, the segment discusses fitting models in high-dimensional space, emphasizing an intuitive geometric approach to understanding the relationship between actual and predicted values as complexity increases. Finally, it defines the span of two vectors as the set of all possible linear combinations, representing the surface created by these vectors.

children.title: Understanding the OLS Problem in Regression
children.summary: The segment discusses the objective of Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) in linear regression, emphasizing the goal to minimize the mean squared error of the model defined as Y = X╬Ė. It explains that the aim is to ensure the predicted values are as close as possible to the true values, which applies to all models being trained. Additionally, it mentions minimizing the length of the residual vector, e = Y - ┼Č, as part of the OLS problem.

children.title: Objective Function and OLS Interpretation
children.summary: The final objective function is defined as one over n times the square of the length of the residual vector, indicating that minimizing the squared length of the residual vector also minimizes the residual vector itself. The correct interpretation of the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) problem includes minimizing the mean squared error, minimizing the distance between true and predicted values, and minimizing the length of the residual vector, leading to the conclusion that all options presented in the poll are correct. A two-minute break is announced before continuing with the calculation and geometric interpretation of the optimal vector.

children.title: Model Fitting in High-Dimensional Space
children.summary: The focus is on fitting a model to find the optimal set of parameters that minimize the residual vector and enhance prediction accuracy. The discussion emphasizes an intuitive and geometric approach rather than vector calculus, highlighting the relationship between the actual values (Y vector) and predicted values in an n-dimensional space. As the dataset and feature count increase, the complexity of the model also rises, making it challenging to visualize and understand the dynamics within this higher-dimensional context.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Okay, so let's restart and talk about the next piece. So the next piece that I promise is going to be how to fit a model. How can we find that the optimum set of petavectors or petave values that are going to give us the smallest residual vector and the best predictions possible. Again, I'm not going to do vector calculus, but instead we're going to go with just intuition and geometric derivation of what's happening in this end-dimensional space that we're dealing with because we're in the end-dimensional space. What we have is that we have our Y vector as an end-dimensional vector. It has prediction for it has ground-fished values for all of the data points that we have or all of the observations and for each one of the observations we also have predicted values. So we have an end-dimensional vector and another end-dimensional vector that we're trying to make sure either as close to each other as possible. So we are in end-dimensional space, in a sense. And as you can imagine, as the size of the data set grows and as the number of features also grows, then the complexity of this model is going to grow and we're going to be in this higher dimensional space, which is hard to directly understand what's happening in it. children.title: Understanding Span in Linear Algebra
children.summary: The span of two vectors is defined as the set of all possible linear combinations of those vectors. It represents the surface created by these vectors when positioned from a common starting point. By adjusting the weights applied to each vector, one can reach any point on this surface. The span of the columns of a matrix X, denoted span(X), is also referred to as the column space, and it forms a subspace of the corresponding vector space.

children.title: Span and Column Space in Linear Algebra
children.summary: The span of two vectors forms a surface, and in the context of a matrix, it refers to the subspace created by linear combinations of its column vectors, known as the column space. This column space, denoted as span(X), is a subspace of R^n, where n is the length of each column vector. The segment discusses how the span of the columns consists of all possible linear combinations and emphasizes that it does not cover the entire R^n, indicating limitations based on the matrix's structure. Additionally, it covers how predictions in a model can be calculated using linear algebra by multiplying rows of a matrix by a column vector or features with their corresponding parameters, and highlights that predictions exist within the span of the matrix X, defined by applying specific weights to the column vectors.

children.title: Understanding Span and Column Space in Linear Algebra
children.summary: The span of two vectors forms a surface, which is referred to as the span of those vectors. When discussing a matrix, the span refers to the subspace created by linear combinations of its column vectors. This concept generalizes the idea of span from two vectors in three-dimensional space to matrices. The span of a matrix, denoted as span(X), represents the set of all possible linear combinations of its columns, also known as the column space. This column space is a subspace of R^n, where n is the length of each column vector.

children.title: Understanding the Span of a Matrix
children.summary: The span of the columns of a matrix X, also referred to as the column space, consists of all possible linear combinations of these columns. This concept implies that if each column of X is n-dimensional, then the span(X) forms a subspace of R^n. The discussion emphasizes that the span does not encompass the entire R^n, highlighting limitations in achieving certain vectors or dimensions based on the matrix's structure.

children.title: Calculating Predictions and Linear Combinations
children.summary: The discussion focuses on calculating predictions in a model using linear algebra concepts. It explains that predictions can be derived by multiplying rows of a matrix by a column vector or by multiplying each feature with its corresponding parameter. The segment emphasizes graphing individual features and their relationships with parameters to obtain predictions. Additionally, it introduces the concept of the span of the columns of a matrix X, which represents all possible linear combinations of those columns, forming a subspace in IRn.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It is a subset of R3 and not the entire R3 that I have. So it helpful in our case when we're talking about how the model is going to do production. It is helpful because if you think about how I was getting all the predictions, there was one way that you would think about the fact that I'm getting all the predictions by multiplying these rows by this column vector. There's also another way that you can think about me calculating each one of these or getting all of these predictions by waiting or by multiplying this VAT factor to each one of these column vectors, or each one of these features that I have. So I would say that in order for me to get all of the predictions for all of the observations, let's go ahead and graph feature one, the column for feature one, multiply all of those feature ones with the parameter teta zero. children.title: Understanding Predictions in Linear Models
children.summary: The discussion clarifies the simplification of predictions using a two-column model, emphasizing that predictions, represented as a vector, exist within the span of the matrix X. The prediction process involves applying specific weights to column vectors, which defines the span of X. The explanation also touches on the concept of an n-dimensional space where multiple features contribute to the prediction values for each data point.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I kind of oversimplified it here. There's no teta zero, there's no other column. This is just a simplification of this case, assuming if you only have two columns. And this way, if you're thinking about getting all the predictions using a way that combination of columns, in a sense, your y hat is going to be a vector that's going to be in the span of your entire matrix X. Again, it's in the span of matrix X because of the way that I can think about how the predictions are being calculated. The predictions are being calculated by these specific weights that I have that are applied to the column vectors. And that's the definition of a span of X. So exactly what's happening, when I'm doing the prediction, is that I'm living in this end-dimensional space. I have multiple different features. that this is feature one, for example, that is end-dimensional. So for each one of the data points that you have, you have a value for feature one. children.title: Linear Prediction and Column Space Concepts
children.summary: Each feature in a dataset is represented as a vector in an n-dimensional space. Predictions from a linear model are confined to the span of the feature matrix X, which includes all linear combinations of its columns, known as the column space. The prediction Y is a linear combination of these columns, residing within the span of X, even if the actual values of Y do not. The goal is to find the vector in span(X) that is closest to the true values of Y, minimizing the vertical distance geometrically. The segment also discusses orthogonal projection, explaining that the prediction vector (Y hat) should be the orthogonal projection of actual values (Y) onto the span of X's columns, minimizing the distance between Y and Y hat. This relationship can be visualized as Y hat being directly beneath Y, similar to a shadow cast by a light bulb. The objective is to minimize the length of the residual vector (Y - Y-hat), where Y-hat is represented as X times theta.

children.title: Linear Prediction and Column Space Concepts
children.summary: Each feature in a dataset can be represented as a vector in an n-dimensional space. The predictions made by a linear model are confined to the span of the feature matrix X, which includes all possible linear combinations of its columns. This span, also known as the column space, represents the set of vectors that can be formed using the columns of X. The prediction Y is a linear combination of these columns, meaning it resides within the span of X, even if the actual values of Y do not. The objective is to find the vector in span(X) that is closest to the true values of Y.

You can think about this is kind of oversimplification of it in kind of 3D, but you can think about each one of the features is going to be a vector in end-dimensional space. All of the Y hats... Vectors that I can get are going to live in the system of X because I know based on the definition that I can the span of X includes all of the possible vectors or positions that I can achieve by a combination of all of the columns. And I know that's exactly what a linear model is able to do. And it cannot achieve anything beyond the span of x that we're living in. So effectively, what that means is that if this is the two columns that I have in my data frame, all of the predictions that I can have with any linear model is going to live on this specific surface. up here right and I'm not able to just do any prediction above. children.title: Linear Combinations and Column Space
children.summary: The segment discusses the concept of the span of the columns of matrix X, which represents all possible linear combinations of those columns, referred to as the column space. It highlights that while predictions (Y) are linear combinations of the columns of X, the true values may lie outside this span. The goal is to find the vector within the span of X that is closest to the actual target vector Y, minimizing the vertical distance in a geometric interpretation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But what's happening is that a lot of times this Y vector or the ground truth values. live outside of the span of x. So just to show you with this kind of physical example that I have imagine that those are my two columns and imagine that for example. Say, for example, one of the lights here is going to be the target valley or the target vector that I want to be in. Right? So effectively, what I want to do is I want to find a weighted combination of all of these features that is as close as possible to this target vector. But I can basically make up for this vertical distance. What I can do is just walk on this surface, figure out where is the best place that is as close as possible to this light, which what I know or what from our intuition we know is that the best place is going to be right under it, so that we don't need to compensate or get penalized for the delta in x and y, but we only get penalized for the delta in z that we have. children.title: Orthogonal Projection and Linear Prediction
children.summary: The segment discusses the concept of orthogonal projection in relation to linear prediction. It explains that the prediction vector (Y hat) should be the orthogonal projection of the actual values (Y) onto the span of the columns of matrix X. This means that the distance between Y and Y hat is minimized, and the relationship can be visualized as Y hat being directly beneath Y, akin to a light bulb casting a shadow. The span of X, defined as the set of all possible linear combinations of its columns, is described as a subspace where the prediction Y will reside, even if the true values Y do not.

children.title: Minimizing Residuals and Orthogonal Projections
children.summary: The objective is to minimize the length of the residual vector (Y - Y-hat), where Y-hat is represented as X times theta. This minimization occurs when the residual vector is the orthogonal projection of Y onto the subspace spanned by X, ensuring that the residual is perpendicular to this subspace. The concept of orthogonal projection in 2D is defined as two vectors being perpendicular, indicated by a dot product of zero. The goal is to minimize the L2 norm of the residual vector to achieve predictions that are as close as possible to the true Y values.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And it is minimized at the place that this delta vector Y minus Y hat is minimized or is orthogonal to the entire span of X. So this is kind of that... Most of the take-aways that you want to take from the lecture today is going to be on this graph. Effectively, it means that the objective function that we have been trying to minimize is that we have been trying to minimize the lengths of this residual vector or y- minus y-hat. There is y, y is right here. There is y-hat, y-hat is right here, or I write it as x times theta. And in order to minimize this distance, I want to make sure that the length of this vector is minimized. And how can I make sure that the length of this vector is minimized? This vector should be the orthogonal projection of y unto this subspace that I'm living in. So we're going to formally define what this orthogonal projection means. And orthogonal projection, when you have two vectors in 2D actually means that they're perpendicular, meaning that the dot product between them is equal to 0. Video: 1: v

leaf_chunk.audio: And it is minimized at the place that this delta vector Y minus Y hat is minimized or is orthogonal to the entire span of X. So this is kind of that... Most of the take-aways that you want to take from the lecture today is going to be on this graph. Effectively, it means that the objective function that we have been trying to minimize is that we have been trying to minimize the lengths of this residual vector or y- minus y-hat. There is y, y is right here. There is y-hat, y-hat is right here, or I write it as x times theta. And in order to minimize this distance, I want to make sure that the length of this vector is minimized. And how can I make sure that the length of this vector is minimized? This vector should be the orthogonal projection of y unto this subspace that I'm living in. So we're going to formally define what this orthogonal projection means. And orthogonal projection, when you have two vectors in 2D actually means that they're perpendicular, meaning that the dot product between them is equal to 0.

children.title: Orthogonality, Dot Product, and OLS Derivation
children.summary: The dot product of two vectors A and B is defined as the product of their norms and the cosine of the angle between them, indicating that a dot product of zero signifies perpendicular vectors. This concept extends to matrices, where a vector is orthogonal to the span of a matrix if it is orthogonal to each of its columns, expressed as M^T * v = 0. Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) estimation is explained, detailing that when the residual vector meets certain criteria, it indicates orthogonality and represents the minimum distance. The optimal estimate, theta hat, is derived from the equation X^T * (Y - X * theta hat) = 0, confirming that the residual vector Y - X is orthogonal to the span of the design matrix X. The derivation of theta hat is discussed, showing that teta hat = (X^T * X)^-1 * X^T * Y is valid only if X^T * X is invertible, emphasizing the condition for invertibility and its implications for solution uniqueness. The segment also summarizes the linear regression process from data frame creation to performance evaluation and highlights the significance of residual analysis in multiple linear regression, stressing the need to plot residuals against predicted values to check for patterns that may indicate issues like heteroscedasticity.

children.title: Orthogonality, Dot Product, and OLS Estimation
children.summary: The dot product of two vectors A and B is defined as the product of their norms and the cosine of the angle between them, indicating that a dot product of zero signifies perpendicular vectors. This concept extends to matrices, where a vector is orthogonal to the span of a matrix if it is orthogonal to each of its columns. In linear algebra, a vector v is orthogonal to a matrix M if the dot product with each column is zero, which can be expressed as M^T * v = 0. The segment also covers Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) estimation, explaining that when the residual vector meets certain criteria, it indicates orthogonality and represents the minimum distance. The optimal estimate, theta hat, is derived from the equation X^T * (Y - X * theta hat) = 0, confirming that the residual vector Y - X is orthogonal to the span of the design matrix X. The normal equation in OLS shows that if the residual vector is orthogonal to the span of the design matrix, then the equation X^T * Y = X^T * X * theta hat holds.

children.title: Understanding Orthogonality and Dot Product
children.summary: The dot product of two vectors A and B is defined as the product of their norms and the cosine of the angle between them. When the angle is 90 degrees, the cosine is zero, resulting in a dot product of zero, which indicates that the vectors are perpendicular. This concept extends to orthogonality in relation to a matrix: a vector is orthogonal to the span of a matrix if it is orthogonal to each column of the matrix. Consequently, it will also be orthogonal to any linear combination of those columns, making it orthogonal to the entire subspace.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Remember that I was telling you that when you calculate the dot product, this is as if you're calculating the norm of A, and times the norm of B or the lengths of B, times the cosine of the angle between them. when this angle is equal to 90 degrees cosine is going to be equal to zero, that's why the doc product between A and B is going to be equal to zero. That's what we mean by two vectors being perpendicular to each other. Or, tokenality in the case of a vector being or being orthogonal to entire span of a matrix, that means that it should be orthogonal to each one of the columns of the matrix. Again, if it is orthogonal to each one of the columns of x, it is also going to be orthogonal to any linear combination of those columns as well. So it's entirely going to be orthogonal to everything or every vector that leaves in that subspace. Video: 1: Lec 12 - DS100 Fa24 - Ordinary

children.title: Orthogonality in Linear Algebra
children.summary: A vector v is orthogonal to a matrix M if the dot product between v and each column of M is zero. This can be expressed in matrix notation, where the transpose of M multiplied by v should equal zero to confirm orthogonality. This concept generalizes the idea of two vectors being perpendicular in 2D space.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So formally defining what's happening is that if I'm assuming that my matrix is m, I have all of these did be different columns. When I say that b is orthogonal to this matrix m, this means that the dot product between each one of the columns and b should be equal to zero, meaning that column transpose this time, we should be equal to zero. to 0 and I'll do exactly the same trick where I will combine all of these calculations into one vector and then I'll factor out v which effectively here means that in order to make sure that a vector v is orthogonal to a span of a matrix m, we just transpose matrix m multiplied with the vector v and that should be equal to 0. When I say that b is orthogonal to this matrix m, this means that the dot product between each one of the columns and b should be equal to zero, meaning that column transpose this time, we should be equal to zero. to 0 and I'll do exactly the same trick where I will combine all of these calculations into one vector and then I'll factor out v which effectively here means that in order to make sure that a vector v is orthogonal to a span of a matrix m, we just transpose matrix m multiplied with the vector v and that should be equal to 0. children.title: Ordinary Least Squares Estimation and Proof
children.summary: The segment discusses the estimating equation for Ordinary Least Squares (OLS), emphasizing that when the residual vector satisfies certain criteria, it indicates orthogonality and represents the minimum distance. The speaker notes that the optimal estimate, denoted as theta hat, can be derived by setting the equation X transpose times (Y - X times theta hat) equal to zero. This establishes that the residual vector Y - X is orthogonal to the span of the design matrix X, which is crucial for minimizing the objective function R(theta). The proof highlights the relationship between the least squares estimate and the properties of the residual vector.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So I'm going to use exactly the same equation. This is going to be my estimating equation, right? Because as soon as my vector, my residual vector meets this criteria, That means that it was orthogonal. That means that was the minimum distance I could have had. And then that means that this tetra that gave me that Y-hat exactly is going to be my optimum tetra-hat. Right? So that's my kind of estimating equation. You can say that one side is equal to zero. This is kind of equal to me taking the derivative setting of the equal to zero that we have been doing previously. So let's go ahead and do that actually. So I'm going to go ahead and say that this vector of y minus y hat or y minus x times peta, um, hat is going to uh X transpose times this vector is going to be equal to 0. Again, because my matrix right now is going to be my design matrix. So I'm going to say X transpose times Y minus X times theta hat is equal to 0. Video: 1: Lec 12 - DS100 Fa24 - Ordinary

children.title: Normal Equation and Orthogonality in OLS
children.summary: The segment discusses the normal equation in ordinary least squares (OLS), which states that if the residual vector is orthogonal to the span of the design matrix, then x transpose times y equals x transpose times x times theta hat. Rearranging this gives the equation x transpose times x times theta hat equal to x transpose times y, which is crucial for solving for the theta values. Additionally, it defines the least squares estimate as the parameter that minimizes the objective function R(theta), where R(theta) is related to the norm of the residual vector.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And again, this is x transpose multiplied with the residual vector. If that is equal to zero, that means that the residual vector is orthogonal to the entire the span of the design matrix x. So I'm just going to go ahead and rearrange some of these terms. So if I rearrange the terms, this is going to be equal to x transpose times y is equal to x transpose times x times theta hat. Okay. Actually, sorry, I just wanted to distribute it, but let me go once that back. x transpose times y minus x transpose times x times theta hat is equal to zero. And then I'll take one term to the other side, the negative term to the other side. This is going to give me x transpose times x times theta hat equal to x transpose times y. This is called normal equation because it's the equation that you can directly solve in order to get all of the tetahed values that be one. Video: 1: Lec 12 - DS100 Fa24 - Ordinary

children.title: Ordinary Least Squares Derivation and Residual Analysis
children.summary: The segment covers the derivation of the optimum parameter vector (teta hat) in ordinary least squares regression, detailing the process of multiplying both sides of the equation by the inverse of the design matrix (X transpose times X) to simplify the solution to teta hat = (X transpose times X inverse times X transpose times Y). It emphasizes that this closed-form solution is valid only if X transpose times X is invertible. The discussion includes the condition for invertibility and its implications for the uniqueness of solutions, along with a summary of the linear regression process from data frame creation to performance evaluation. Additionally, it addresses residual analysis in multiple linear regression, highlighting the importance of plotting residuals against predicted values and ensuring no patterns emerge, as patterns may indicate issues like heteroscedasticity.

children.title: Ordinary Least Squares Solution Derivation
children.summary: The segment explains how to derive the optimum values for the parameter vector (teta hat) in ordinary least squares regression. By multiplying both sides of the equation by the inverse of the design matrix (X transpose times X), the solution simplifies to teta hat being equal to (X transpose times X inverse times X transpose times Y). This closed-form solution exists only if X transpose times X is invertible. Additionally, the segment discusses the least squares estimate that minimizes the objective function R(0) and the condition that the residual vector (Y - X beta) is orthogonal to the span of X, leading to the normal equation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And as soon as you have x and you have y, you can solve this set of equations that you have right here in order to find out what is the optimum tetahed values. will take you one one step further we would say that what if I multiply both sides by x transpose times x inverse? If I multiply both sides by x transpose times x inverse, then on the left hand side, I am going to be left with only teta hat. So this is going to be teta hat equal to x transpose times x inverse times x transpose times y. And this is the final solution for how to arrive at the optimum set of Heta values in the Teta vector having had the design matrix X and then having had your target values Y vector. And this is a close form solution for it. And this close-from-solution exists if and only if x transpose times x is invertible. Video: 1: Lec 12 - DS100 Fa24 - Ordinary

children.title: Invertibility Condition in Linear Regression
children.summary: The segment discusses the condition for the invertibility of X transpose times X in the context of linear regression. If X transpose times X is not invertible, it indicates that there is no unique solution, although a solution still exists. This leads to a summary of the entire process of linear regression, including the creation of a data frame, calculating the cost function, and determining the theta hat vector using input features. The evaluation of the model's performance is also mentioned, along with a demonstration using an NDA data set.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And we're going to talk about... What is the condition that X transpose times X is going to be invertible? If it's not invertible, then in that case we can't actually have this term and we can directly solve it. It means that there is not going to be any unique solution, but there will be a solution. So, this is kind of a clean version of this. This is the final kind of last piece of this linear regression or multiple linear regression that we wanted to see that deserves one final slide or one separate slide by itself. So, kind of a summary of everything to be seen so far and kind of evaluating the performance of these models. There is a demo that we have that actually looks at this NDA data set. Go through all of this entire cycle. It looks that, okay, we're creating the data frame. We're creating. We're calculating also this cost function and we're going to calculate also this theta hat vector using all of these input input features that we have. And then at the end, it will go ahead and do the evaluation. Video: 1: Lec 12 - DS100 Fa24 - Ordinary

children.title: Residual Analysis in Multiple Linear Regression
children.summary: In multiple linear regression, the evaluation of the model uses the residual vector, which should ideally not exhibit any patterns. Unlike simple linear regression, where residuals are plotted against a single feature, in multiple linear regression, residuals are plotted against the predicted values (Y hat). It is important to ensure that there is no discernible pattern in these residuals, as patterns indicate issues such as heteroscedasticity, where model performance varies across the range of predicted values. For instance, if residuals are closer to zero for lower Y hat values but show over- and under-predictions as Y increases, this suggests non-uniform model performance.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So in terms of the evaluation, previously, we had seen that we would do evaluation using the residual vector. So remember that the residual vector in simple linear regression should not have shown any kind of pattern. But if we go ahead and want to visualize the residual vector in our case, we won't have a specific feature to use in the x-axis, because we have multiple different features. Instead, the residual vector version that we're showing with multiple linear regression is always going to be showing the residual with respect to the Y hat values instead of with respect to each one of the individual features that we have. Still, we don't want to see any pattern. If we see any pattern, meaning that, okay, for example, in this case, it will perform better for Y hat, so there are smaller. So the values, the residuals are close to zero, but as the value of Y grows, then I'll love these, there's over predictions and under predictions that are happening. This is called Petra Sidescity, meaning that the performance of the model is not uniform across the Y axis or the Y-hat axis that we have. Video: 1: v

children.title: Coefficient of Determination in Regression Models
children.summary: The segment discusses the evaluation of models using root-minus-squared error and correlation to assess linear relationships between features and target values. In multiple linear regression, the coefficient of determination (R┬▓) is introduced as a measure of variance captured by the model, calculated as the variance of fitted values divided by the variance of ground-truth values. A higher R┬▓ indicates better model performance, with examples given showing how adding features increases R┬▓, approaching 1 when all relevant features are included, thus explaining the variance in the dataset effectively.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Again, we don't want to see a pattern in this case, regardless. We also calculate that root-minus-squared error in order to evaluate models last time with simple linear regression and constant model. We also looked at the correlation in order to measure that the models are linearly, or the feature is linearly related to the target value that we have. In the multiple linear regression, what we'll use is coefficient of determination, which is a fraction that we're calculating that talks about what is the variance of the fitted values divided by the variance of the ground-trose values. And S is what it does is it will tell you that what percent, what proportion of the variance in the underlying data are you capturing. So we want to make sure that we capture as much as possible of the underlying variance in the data so that we modeled everything and all the pattern that happened in the data. We can actually go ahead and if you take a look at the demo of notebook what you will see is that if I go ahead and just have a model that is a simple linear regression that just does the prediction based on the ASD feature the coefficient of determination is going to be 0.45 and as you keep adding features for example here I'm adding features of ASD and I'm using features of ASD and TRIP8 then the coefficient of determination is going to get To go higher in fact if you try and all of those three features together the coefficient of determination is going to be very close to one meaning that you explain the entire variance that you had in the entire data set that you have had. leaf_chunk.audio: Again, we don't want to see a pattern in this case, regardless. We also calculate that root-minus-squared error in order to evaluate models last time with simple linear regression and constant model. We also looked at the correlation in order to measure that the models are linearly, or the feature is linearly related to the target value that we have. In the multiple linear regression, what we'll use is coefficient of determination, which is a fraction that we're calculating that talks about what is the variance of the fitted values divided by the variance of the ground-trose values. And S is what it does is it will tell you that what percent, what proportion of the variance in the underlying data are you capturing. So we want to make sure that we capture as much as possible of the underlying variance in the data so that we modeled everything and all the pattern that happened in the data. We can actually go ahead and if you take a look at the demo of notebook what you will see is that if I go ahead and just have a model that is a simple linear regression that just does the prediction based on the ASD feature the coefficient of determination is going to be 0.45 and as you keep adding features for example here I'm adding features of ASD and I'm using features of ASD and TRIP8 then the coefficient of determination is going to get To go higher in fact if you try and all of those three features together the coefficient of determination is going to be very close to one meaning that you explain the entire variance that you had in the entire data set that you have had.

children.title: Properties of OLS and Linear Models
children.summary: The properties of the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) model include the orthogonality of the residual vector to the span of X, leading to key results such as the product of the transpose of X and the residual vector equaling zero. The prediction vector's transpose times the residual vector also equals zero, as the prediction vector lies within the span of X. Homework will require proving that the summation of the residuals equals zero, derived from the residual's orthogonality to the column of ones in the design matrix. For linear models with an intercept, the sum of residuals is zero, which prevents their direct use as an objective function; instead, absolute values and scores of residuals are calculated. The OLS equation is expressed as ╬▓╠é = X ╬Ė, with a unique solution existing if and only if X^T X is invertible. Further properties and implications will be discussed in the next lecture.

children.title: Properties of OLS and Residuals
children.summary: The properties of the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) model include the orthogonality of the residual vector to the span of X, which leads to several key results. It will be shown that the product of the transpose of X and the residual vector equals zero. Additionally, the prediction vector's transpose times the residual vector also equals zero since the prediction vector is within the span of X. Homework will require proving that the summation of the residuals equals zero, which follows from the residual's orthogonality to the column of ones in the design matrix.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So there are some properties of this OLS or this model that we derived today. You're going to be simple. Some of them you're going to prove in the homework. But effectively, all of them are going to be derived from the fact that the residual vector is orthogonal to the span of X. So you're going to go ahead and basically see that this is the same property that we had that x transpose times residual should be equal to zero. And from that point, you will also be able to prove that the prediction vector transpose times residual vector should also be equal to zero because the prediction vector is also in this span of x. You will also prove in the homework that the summation of the residuals is equal to zero Because the residual was orthogonal to each one of the columns, it is also orthogonal to this column of all the values of 1s. And if you actually write it, this is going to basically give you that the summation of the residual values is equal to 1. Video: 1: v

leaf_chunk.audio: So there are some properties of this OLS or this model that we derived today. You're going to be simple. Some of them you're going to prove in the homework. But effectively, all of them are going to be derived from the fact that the residual vector is orthogonal to the span of X. So you're going to go ahead and basically see that this is the same property that we had that x transpose times residual should be equal to zero. And from that point, you will also be able to prove that the prediction vector transpose times residual vector should also be equal to zero because the prediction vector is also in this span of x. You will also prove in the homework that the summation of the residuals is equal to zero Because the residual was orthogonal to each one of the columns, it is also orthogonal to this column of all the values of 1s. And if you actually write it, this is going to basically give you that the summation of the residual values is equal to 1.

children.title: Properties of Linear Models with Intercept
children.summary: For linear models with an intercept term, the sum of residuals is zero, which is why residuals are not used directly as an objective function; instead, absolute values and scores of residuals are calculated. The ordinary least squares equation is expressed as ╬▓╠é = X ╬Ė, with a unique solution existing if and only if X^T X is invertible. Further properties and implications of these concepts will be discussed in the next lecture.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I'm going to leave you with this fact that because the residual, the summation of the residuals is equal to 0, when there is a bias term, that's why we didn't use the residuals directly as an objective function. And we always calculated absolute value and also scores of the residuals. So we're going to stop here. There are more properties and the summation of every kind of model that you'd seen in all of the linear models. The last piece is that with the ordinary least square, the equation is always going to be by had equal to x times teta vector that derived actually how we can calculate the teta hat. And then we also derive this fact that there is a unique solution if and only if x transpose times x is invertible. Thank you so much for coming. We will continue this in the next lecture.
